RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxy-3-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-[[(2R,3R,4R,5R,6S)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxymethyl]oxan-2-yl]oxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Rutin, is a naturally occurring flavonoid glycoside found in plants, known for its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and vasoprotective properties; Rutin is widely used as a dietary supplement and medicinal agent due to its potent antioxidant and free-radical scavenging activities, it stabilizes blood vessels, reduces capillary fragility, may protect against oxidative stress-related diseases, clinically it is known for its Vitamin P activity, primarily used to strengthen capillary walls, reduce capillary permeability, and treat conditions like varicose veins, hemorrhoids, and capillary fragility, it also exhibits anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties, and its poor water solubility limits bioavailability, but derivatives are explored for enhanced delivery.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (2S,3S,4R,5R,6R)-5-amino-2-(aminomethyl)-6-[(2R,3S,4R,5S)-5-[(1R,2R,3S,5R,6S)-3,5-diamino-2-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3-amino-4,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]oxy-6-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-4-hydroxy-2-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-3-yl]oxyoxane-3,4-diol;sulfuric acid, also referred to as Paromomycin sulphate, is an aminoglycoside antibiotic existing as a sulfate salt, and Paromomycin sulphate is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds to the 16S rRNA of bacterial ribosomes, causing misreading of mRNA and inhibiting protein synthesis and is used to treat parasitic and bacterial infections, its core structure, (2S,3S,4R,5R,6R)-5-amino-2-(aminomethyl)-6-[(2R,3S,4R,5S)-5-[(1R,2R,3S,5R,6S)-3,5-diamino-2-[(2S,3R,4R,5S,6R)-3-amino-4,5-dihydroxy-6-(hydroxymethyl)oxan-2-yl]oxy-6-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-4-hydroxy-2-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-3-yl]oxyoxane-3,4-diol, is similar to (2R,3S,4R,5R,6R)-5-amino-2-(aminomethyl)-6-[(1R,2R,3S,4R,6S)-4,6-diamino-2-[(2S,3R,4S,5R)-4-[(2R,3R,4R,5S,6S)-3-amino-6-(aminomethyl)-4,5-dihydroxyoxan-2-yl]oxy-3-hydroxy-5-(hydroxymethyl)oxolan-2-yl]oxy-3-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxyoxane-3,4-diol, consisting of multiple aminosugar units linked via glycosidic bonds, featuring numerous amino and hydroxyl groups, the presence of multiple amino groups makes it strongly polycationic, which is the structural basis for its biological activity, allowing it to specifically bind to the bacterial ribosome and disrupt protein synthesis, its formulation as a sulfate salt is typical for this class of drugs, enhancing its water solubility and stability for clinical applications such as injection or solution preparation, this molecule is highly likely to be a clinically relevant antibiotic, such as a derivative of streptomycin, kanamycin, or tobramycin, employed as a broad-spectrum bactericidal agent primarily against aerobic Gram-negative infections, with caution required due to the risk of ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity.

RNA information: The expanded RNA repeat r(G₄C₂)ₑₓₚ, the genetic cause of c9ALS/FTD, dynamically folds between a G-quadruplex and a hairpin structure featuring 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loops. The hairpin form is the predominant and functionally relevant structure within cells, where it mediates disease pathogenesis by two key mechanisms: it sequesters RNA-binding proteins into nuclear foci, and it undergoes repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to generate neurotoxic dipeptide repeat proteins. This specific hairpin conformation, particularly its GG internal loops, is thus a critical therapeutic target for ameliorating toxicity, as its selective targeting inhibits both protein sequestration and RAN translation.
Small_molecule_information: 5,11-dimethyl-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazol-9-ol, also referred to as Compound 1 (a potent but non-selective 1a-derivative) and Compound 1e (a bis-benzimidazole derivative that closely resembles 1a in its 9-hydroxy-6H-pyrido[4,3-b]carbazolium core but lacks the cationic piperidinium side chain, carrying instead a neutral 2-hydroxyethyl tail), is a tetracyclic aromatic heterocyclic compound based on the pyrido[4,3-b]carbazole scaffold, which forms the core of the well-known ellipticine alkaloid: the molecule is substituted with methyl groups at positions 5 and 11, and a hydroxyl group at position 9, and its planar conjugated system gives it fluorescence properties, with the molecular formula C19H16N2O; Compound 1 effectively inhibits RAN translation but also modestly affects canonical translation, indicating some off-target effects that limit its therapeutic potential, while Compound 1e has reduced affinity for the pathogenic 5CGG/3GGC internal loops of the r(CGG)exp RNA hairpin and weakened ability to disrupt the toxic r(CGG)12DGCR8 complex, leading to attenuated cellular efficacy (restoring partial wild-type SMN2 and Bcl-x pre-mRNA splicing in FXTAS-model COS-7 cells and achieving a modest reduction in the number of r(CGG)exp nuclear foci at a specific concentration) but retaining low cytotoxicity and good aqueous solubility, making it a useful negative-control probe for dissecting structureactivity relationships and validating the requirement of a permanent positive charge in next-generation ligands targeting CGG-repeat disorders; this compound and its derivatives are potent Topoisomerase II inhibitors and DNA intercalators, exhibiting strong anticancer activity.

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[2-(4-fluoro-3-methoxyphenyl)ethenyl]-1-methyl-4-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)quinolin-1-ium, also referred to as Compound 18, is a quinoline quaternary ammonium salt compound bearing a permanent positive charge, which is a 1-methylquinolinium derivative with a 4-fluoro-3-methoxystyryl group at C2 and a 4-methylpiperazin-1-yl moiety at C4 of the quinoline ring and is synthesized via modular condensation and quaternization, its core scaffold is the 1-methyl-4-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)quinolin-1-ium cation, linked via a vinylene bridge to a 4-fluoro-3-methoxyphenyl group, the quaternary ammonium salt structure ensures the molecule is positively charged at any pH, predisposing it to bind negatively charged biomolecules like DNA or cell membrane phospholipids, it can bind ADAM10 mRNAs G-quadruplex with selectivity over mutant RNA, while it weakly enhances ADAM10 translation, it lacks robust activity, unlike analog 24, due to suboptimal interactions with the RNAs G-rich 5-UTR inhibitory motif, the long conjugated system provides the molecule with strong fluorescent characteristics, in reality, such cationic quinoline dyes are widely used for live cell imaging, particularly for selective staining of mitochondria or nucleic acids, its permanent positive charge also makes it a potential antibacterial or anticancer agent, acting by interfering with membrane potential or nucleic acid function.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(E,2S,7S)-1,8-bis[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-7-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-1,8-dioxooct-4-en-2-yl]-2-ethyl-N-methylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide is a highly similar, N-methylated poly-cationic peptidomimetic to N-[(E,2S)-1,8-bis[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-7-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-1,8-dioxooct-4-en-2-yl]-2-ethyl-N-methylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide, but possesses (2S,7S) chirality in the core oct-4-enediamide scaffold, giving it a symmetrical structure. The molecule maintains all the N-methylation modifications and poly-cationic characteristics. The presence of dual symmetry is often intended to achieve a more defined and regular three-dimensional conformation with fewer conformational isomers. This structural rigidity is crucial for precise interaction with highly ordered biological targets, such as DNA or protein-protein interfaces. N-[(E,2S,7S)-1,8-bis[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-7-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-1,8-dioxooct-4-en-2-yl]-2-ethyl-N-methylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide is a promising candidate for a high-performance peptidomimetic drug with enhanced target selectivity.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-6-phenyl-N-[3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl]thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as compound 4, is a fused heterocyclic amide based on the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine scaffold, and it is also a 3-aminothieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide derivative characterized by a phenyl substituent at the 6-position, a structural modification critical for its enhanced potency as a TAR RNA binder. This tricyclic backbone features an amino group at position 3 and a phenyl group at position 6. It is linked via an amide bond to a 3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl substituent. The molecule is highly lipophilic, thanks to the phenyl group and the crucial trifluoromethyl (CF3) group. The trifluoromethyl group is a classic substituent in medicinal chemistry, used to enhance metabolic stability and transmembrane permeability, while also acting as a strong electron-withdrawing group. This planar, highly lipophilic amidic heterocycle is a prime example of a small molecule drug, often investigated as a non-competitive or competitive inhibitor of signal pathway enzymes like tyrosine kinases, with broad applicability in anti-cancer, immunomodulatory, and anti-inflammatory fields; additionally, it specifically interacts with key nucleobases in the HIV-1 TAR RNA elementG21, U23, and G43demonstrating dual binding affinity for both the bulge region (U23) and the lower stem (G43), a unique interaction profile that establishes it as a promising lead molecule for developing antiviral therapeutics that target RNA-protein interactions.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N-[2-(dimethylamino)ethyl]-2-[[11-[2-(dimethylamino)ethylamino]-5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-7-yl]oxy]acetamide iodide, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine or quaternary ammonium salt side chains. This structural class is known for DNA/RNA binding and topoisomerase inhibition, with many such compounds developed as broad-spectrum anticancer agents. Real-world applications may involve their use as novel anti-tumor leads or fluorescent probes for cancer therapy or biological research.

Small_molecule_information: N-[(2S)-1-[(2S,4R)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]-4-hydroxypyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S,4R)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]-4-hydroxypyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[methyl(quinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-N-methylquinoline-3-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 7, is a complex peptide analog of N-[(2S)-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[methyl(quinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-N-methylquinoline-3-carboxamide, featuring a 4-hydroxypyrrolidine group to potentially enhance water solubility or metabolic stability, and Compound 7 is a hydroxyproline-bearing analog similar in concept to compound 5 but optimized with alternative structural adjustments, it is a candidate for biologically active peptide drugs, Compound 7 bound the HIV-1 FSS RNA with strong affinity, significantly stronger than compound 5 and closer to compound 6, selectivity assays confirmed preferential binding to FSS over bulk RNA, in cellular assays, compound 7 displayed antiviral activity, reducing HIV-1 production with dose dependence, although it did not achieve complete viral suppression within the assay timeframe, Compound 7 therefore represents a favorable balance between functional group modification and maintained high-affinity RNA targeting, supporting hydroxyprolines context-dependent value.

RNA information: 5′UAG/3′A_C (SL I): The UAG/A_C motif is located within HCV 3′ UTR stem–loop I. Identified in the Inforna analysis as a ligandable element, it was enriched in binding assays, indicating its potential as a structural determinant for small molecule recognition and functional viral RNA inhibition.
Small_molecule_information: 1-N,3-N,5-N-tris[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxamide, also referred to as Compound 8, is a molecule with a trimeric structure, built upon a benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxamide core, which is a rigid, three-armed scaffold with C3h symmetry. The end of each arm is substituted with a phenyl group bearing a 4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl (imidazolinyl) group. Consequently, the entire molecule contains a total of three imidazolinyl groups, which are strong bases that can be protonated at physiological pH, making it a potent tricationic system. This rigid, symmetrical, polycationic architecture is highly suitable for acting as a high-affinity DNA binding agent and also displays strong selectivity for RNA internal loops, especially motifs containing 5UUA/3A_U, 5GCU/3C_A, and 5UCUAU/3ACCA, with its recognition pattern highlighting tolerance for mismatched and bulged bases within short hairpins; the molecule effectively binds HCV 3 UTR stemloops I and II, consistent with motif enrichment analyses, and cellular studies revealed potent inhibition of HCV replication, with ASO competition confirming direct binding to viral RNA motifs. Its three-armed structure may allow it to interact simultaneously with multiple sites on the DNA helix, such as two minor groove sites and a phosphate backbone site. Compounds of this type are frequently designed in medicinal chemistry as anticancer, antitrypanosomal agents, or as gene transfection aids, and these results position this molecule as a promising lead for selective RNA-targeted antiviral development.

RNA information: Ca.LSU (Candida albicans Large Subunit) is a self-splicing intron of Class I located in the ribosome large subunit of the Candida albicans 26S rRNA gene. It plays a key role in the maturation process of rRNA, removing itself from the rRNA precursor through the self-splicing mechanism, thereby allowing for further processing and maturation of rRNA. The structure and function of Ca.LSU are of vital importance in the biosynthesis of ribosomes. Its correct folding and catalytic activity are crucial for the maturation of rRNA and the assembly of ribosomes. Studies have shown that certain compounds, such as pentamidine, can inhibit the self-splicing activity of Ca.LSU by binding to specific regions of introns and altering their folding structure. This inhibitory effect can be partially or completely reversed by adding positively charged molecules such as magnesium ions, calcium ions or arginine, which indicates that these positively charged molecules compete with pentamidine for binding sites, protecting the normal folding and function of introns.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[3-(4-carbamimidoylphenoxy)propoxy]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Propamidine, is also a bis-amidine ether, but with a shorter propoxy ether linker chain (Propamidine molecules are relatively short and consist only of para-amidino-phenoxy groups connected by three methylene Bridges, presenting a short-arm diamidine configuration, whose length and flexibility limit the deep insertion within the RNA groove, allowing only loose binding to the peripheral asymmetric loop and lacking stable multivalent interactions, thus having almost no significant inhibition on the splicing activity of Class I introns), and similar to 4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenoxy)pentoxy]benzenecarboximidamide, this class of bis-amidine ethers is designed as potential DNA-binding drugs with broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity, and due to the differing chain lengths, the molecules mode of DNA binding, cell membrane permeability, and selectivity against different pathogens may vary.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-2-(4-fluorobenzoyl)-9-methyl-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 7, incorporates a fluorine atom on the benzoyl ring which improved its binding properties, showed good affinity for CAG RNA and was used to study the effect of halogen substituents; this is a complex pentacyclic fused heterocycle based on the chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one scaffold, a fused chromone and pyridine system, featuring an amino group at the 3-position, a phenyl group at the 4-position, a methyl group at the 9-position, and a 4-fluorobenzoyl group at the 2-position, and chromone structures are found in natural products and various anti-inflammatory and antiallergic drugs, while this rigid, multi-ring, highly substituted structure suggests potential as a kinase or phosphodiesterase (PDE) inhibitor candidate, potentially for anti-cancer or anti-inflammatory applications, and the 4-fluoro substitution on the benzoyl group is a common modification to improve metabolic stability and lipophilicity.

Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(E)-2-(1H-indol-2-yl)ethenyl]-1-methyl-4-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)quinolin-1-ium, also referred to as Compound 23This is a green-yellow cationic quinolinium derivative and dye featuring a highly conjugated structure with an indole group and a piperazine group, which has a 2-(1H-indol-2-yl)vinyl group at the 2-position and a 4-methylpiperazin-1-yl group at the 4-position of the quinoline ring. Cationic dyes based on quinoline are known for their ability to bind to nucleic acids (DNA/RNA), and this compound has affinity for A10-RNA-Wt (ADAM10 mRNAs G-quadruplex sequence) as shown by SPR assay. Combining quinoline and indole results in strong fluorescence properties. Its real-world activity is primarily as a fluorescent dye for nuclear staining and quantification in cell biology, and due to its strong DNA binding, it also possesses potential broad-spectrum antimicrobial, antifungal, or anticancer activity; in addition, dual-luciferase reporter assay reveals it enhances ADAM10 translation, and it has certain cytotoxicity towards related cells.

RNA information: 5′UCUAU/3′ACCA: This extended loop motif was bound by Compound 8 with relatively weaker affinity . Despite the lower binding strength, its presence in the viral RNA offered additional binding options for ligands, contributing to the diversity of recognized structural elements in HCV 3′ UTR.
Small_molecule_information: 1-N,3-N,5-N-tris[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxamide, also referred to as Compound 8, is a molecule with a trimeric structure, built upon a benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxamide core, which is a rigid, three-armed scaffold with C3h symmetry. RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 165ribD is a riboswitch that can specifically bind to FMN (flavin mononucleotide). It maintains the homeostasis of FMN and riboflavin in bacterial cells by regulating gene expression.
Small_molecule_information: 1-[2-(7,8-dimethyl-2,4-dioxobenzo[g]pteridin-10-yl)ethyl]piperidine-4-carboxylic acid, also referred to as BRX1224, is a flavin analogue with a piperidine carboxylic acid side chain, with the 7,8-dimethylbenzo[g]pteridine-2,4-dione scaffold as its core: the N10 side chain is connected via an ethyl group to the nitrogen atom of the piperidine ring, which bears a carboxylic acid at position 4, with a calculated molecular formula of CHNO; it is a synthetic analog of FMN (flavin mononucleotide) whose structural design involves introducing a heptanoic acid side chain at the end of FMNs ribityl chain and adding a piperidine substituent at the N8 position, aiming to enhance the molecules binding affinity and transcription termination efficiency while reducing dependence on the phosphate group, exhibits high binding affinity indicating efficient binding to the FMN riboswitch and shows strong activity in transcription termination assays, has been proven to effectively induce conformational changes in the riboswitch through SHAPE chemical probing technology, further confirming its ability to bind to the riboswitch, and the research on it provides an important structural basis for the development of more effective antibacterial drugs, particularly in exploring how chemical modifications can enhance antibacterial activity and selectivity, while the combination of piperidine and carboxylic acid provides additional polar and basic centers to the molecule, which may influence its activity in the central nervous system or its potential as a receptor ligand.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2,3-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium, also referred to as Nitidine, is another methylated benzophenanthridine quaternary alkaloid, similar to 1,2-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium but with different methoxy substitution (2,3-positions), and as a benzophenanthridine alkaloid it also possesses antitumor and antimalarial activity, can inhibit topoisomerases and telomerase to disrupt DNA replication, and its fluorescent properties aid in cellular imaging studies; due to its planar, rigid, and positively charged structural features, its primary activity in practice is as a DNA intercalator and cytotoxic agent, exhibiting strong anti-tumor properties, minor changes in the substitution pattern (from 1,2- to 2,3-dimethoxy) are critical in drug design to modulate the molecules charge distribution and steric bulk, which can alter its interaction strength and selectivity for specific DNA sequences or Topoisomerase isozymes, and this makes it valuable in the structure-activity relationship studies of novel anticancer agents.

Small_molecule_information: (3Z)-3-hydroxyimino-2-phenylchromen-4-one, also referred to as CP9 or an oximinoflavone derivative, has the formula C15H11NO3. Its structure is a derivative of flavone (2-phenylchromen-4-one) featuring a chromone (benzopyranone) core with an oxime group and a phenyl ring, where an oxime group (CNOH) is attached at the 3 position, and the oxime bond has a (Z) geometric isomer configuration. This class of compounds is often obtained through chemical synthesis or structural modification of natural flavonoids. In pharmaceutical chemistry research, oxime derivatives of flavonoids are often explored for metal ion chelation or as enzyme inhibitors. This specific molecule and its analogues are studied for their potential antioxidant and antitumor activities, for instance, by inhibiting certain tyrosine kinases or acting as neuroprotective agents. Such oxime-containing molecules are also sometimes used in drug chemistry to improve lipophilicity or alter metabolic stability, though the presence of the oxime in this compound likely contributed to a very high Kd2 value, indicating poor or non-specific binding and disqualifying it as a candidate.

Small_molecule_information: 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, also referred to as Tetrachlorofluorescein, is a spiro compound, joining a benzofuranone ring (or phthalic anhydride moiety) to a xanthene scaffold via a common carbon atom (the spiro carbon), forming a spiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one structure, essentially a derivative of Rhodamine or Fluorescein, and this fluorescent dye derived from fluorescein is also oFTAn used as a tracer in biochemical assays. This molecule belongs to the family of well-known organic fluorescent dyes, characterized by a large conjugated system that allows the molecule to absorb light at a specific wavelength and emit characteristic fluorescence, and it lacks specific RNA/DNA binding properties but serves as a marker in staining applications. In 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro-3,6-dihydroxyspiro[2-benzofuran-3,9-xanthene]-1-one, the xanthene moiety bears 3,6-dihydroxy groups, which facilitate the formation of the emissive quinoid structure, while the 4,5,6,7-tetrachloro substituents on the benzofuranone ring can significantly modify its fluorescence properties, such as increasing quantum yield or causing a red-shift in the emission wavelength. In real-world applications, these compounds are indispensable tools in biomedical research and diagnostics, widely used as fluorescent probes for cell labeling, protein tracking, immunofluorescence staining, and DNA sequencing, and also act as a tracer in biochemical assays and marker in staining applications.

RNA information: Located at positions 36–55 in the KRAS 5′-UTR. It forms a parallel RG4 with Tm = 64°C and ΔG = –3.7 kcal/mol. It binds strongly to BG4 antibody and is highly stabilized by ligands 2a and 2b, showing significant translational repression in luciferase assays.
Small_molecule_information: 4,11-bis(2-aminoethylamino)naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzofuran-5,10-dione, also referred to as a furan-fused anthraquinone derivative with bis(2-aminoethylamino) side chains, is a compound based on a Naphthoquinone core fused with a Benzofuran ring, resulting in a Naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzofuran-5,10-dione structure, substituted with two 2-aminoethylamino groups at positions C-4 and C-11. Anthraquinone/Naphthoquinone compounds are a well-known class of DNA topoisomerase inhibitors and DNA intercalators, forming the basis of many chemotherapy drugs (e.g., Mitoxantrone). Additionally, these quinone structures readily undergo redox cycling, generating Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) that cause DNA damage and cell apoptosis, and this compound also exhibits high cellular uptake, strong stabilization of KRAS RG4s and specific binding affinity, which enables it to effectively inhibit translation and induce apoptosis in pancreatic cancer cells, meaning it is therefore primarily researched as a novel anti-tumor agent with a mechanism involving dual DNA intercalation and ROS-mediated cytotoxicity.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[11-[3-(dimethylamino)propylamino]-5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-7-yl]oxy]ethyl-trimethylazanium diiodide, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine and quaternary ammonium salt side chains. The presence of the quaternary ammonium salt typically confers higher polarity and DNA binding affinity, making it a potential potent anti-tumor agent or a fluorescent probe in biological research.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 1-cyclopropyl-7-(4-ethylpiperazin-1-yl)-6-fluoro-4-oxoquinoline-3-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Enrofloxacin, is a classic Fluoroquinolone antibiotic with N1-cyclopropyl, C6-fluoro, and C7-piperazine substituents (similar to Ciprofloxacin) that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, preventing DNA replication, and is used clinically as a broad-spectrum antimicrobial for infections including those of the respiratory and urinary tracts.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE RNA is a highly structured regulatory element of HIV-1, shaped by specific base-pairing and loop regions. Its conserved binding pocket, with key residues like G70 and specific C residues, specifically binds the viral Rev protein, facilitating nuclear export of unspliced viral mRNAs—critical for HIV replication. It is a promising antiviral target, as synthetic ligands (e.g., thienopyridine derivatives) block the Rev-RRE interaction to inhibit viral propagation.
Small_molecule_information: N-(2-amino-6-methylthieno[2,3-b]pyridin-3-yl)-3-(trifluoromethyl)benzamide, also referred to as Compound 19, an isosteric derivative of the hit compound designed to systematically explore structural alternatives to the 3-amino/2-amide pharmacophore, was synthesized to evaluate the effect of bioisosteric replacement on binding affinity and selectivity, potentially improving metabolic stability or other drug-like properties, and this analog provides critical insights for optimizing the core scaffold in medicinal chemistry; the core structure is an N-(aminothienopyridinyl)benzamide, where the benzamide group is attached to the 3-amino group of the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine ring, with a free amino group at the 2-position, the molecule is substituted with a 3-(trifluoromethyl)benzoyl group, a highly lipophilic group, and this specific positional isomer of the amide bond is important in SAR studies, as it changes the molecules conformation and electronic properties compared to the 2-amide analogs, and this structural type is commonly investigated as a candidate for kinase inhibitors or antimicrobial agents, with its activity dictated by the precise interaction geometry with the target.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(7-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-1-ium-6-yl)-6-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]-1,3-benzothiazole, also referred to as SMSM40, is based on the 1,3-benzothiazole core, an aromatic heterocycle known for its intrinsic fluorescence and applications in oncology and as probes for Alzheimers -amyloid protein, and SMSM40 is a non-planar coumarin analog with a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system, carrying a 1 charge. This compound is a dicationic salt: the 7-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-1-ium group at C2 is quaternized at N1, and the (3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl group at C6 is also quaternized, and SMSM40 recognizes the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics, confirmed by FP and SAR comparison, serving as a selective RNA ligand. This highly aromatic and dicationic structure makes 2-(7-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-1-ium-6-yl)-6-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]-1,3-benzothiazole highly suitable as a high-affinity DNA/RNA binding agent. Given its benzothiazole cores similarity to Alzheimers probes, it may also hold potential for the diagnosis or treatment of amyloidosis.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Myricetin, is a natural flavonol compound with the formula C15H10O8, and is a flavonoid with antioxidant, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects that binds to CAG repeat RNA, inhibiting toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Its structure is a flavonol backbone with hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and crucially, three hydroxyl groups on the B ring (3, 4, and 5 positions, a pyrogallol moiety), and its polyphenolic structure enables metal chelation and free radical scavenging. Myricetin is widely found in fruits, vegetables, berries, wine, and tea. Functionally, possessing six hydroxyl groups, particularly the pyrogallol structure on the B ring, Myricetin is recognized as one of the most powerful natural antioxidants known, capable of efficiently scavenging various free radicals and protecting cells from oxidative stress, and it also exhibits significant anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antitumor, and cardiovascular protective effects, often by modulating signaling pathways and enzyme activities, in addition to its anticancer and neuroprotective properties linked to its binding with CAG repeat RNA and inhibition of toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Due to its potent antioxidant capacity, it is extensively studied and applied in nutritional supplements and natural drug development.

RNA information: xpt-pbuX riboswitch is an guanine-sensing RNA regulatory element located in the 5' untranslated region of Bacillus subtilis mRNA, consisting of a conserved aptamer domain and an expression platform. Its aptamer folds into a three-way connection structure composed of three stems, P1, P2, and P3, and two rings, L2 and L3. The core area precisely recognizes and closely binds guanine through seven hydrogen bonds. After guanine binding, it stabilizes the P1 stem, promotes the formation of a terminator stem in the downstream expression platform, blocks the transcription of the downstream xpt-pbuX gene, and thereby inhibits the expression of purine transporters. In the absence of guanine, the aptamer conformation is loose, and the 3' end of P1 can form an antiterminator structure with the expression platform, allowing transcription to continue. This riboswitch dynamically regulates gene expression by sensing intracellular guanine concentration, maintains purine metabolic balance, and can be activated by artificially designed guanine analogues (such as G7) to achieve antibacterial effects.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(methoxyamino)-1,7-dihydropurin-6-one, also referred to as G2 (a derivative of guanine with an N-methoxy group (-N-OCH) introduced at the C2 position), is a purine derivative related to guanine or hypoxanthine, substituted with methoxyamino at the 2-position; this compound can bind to xpt-pbuX riboswitch aptamer and induce RNA to produce structural changes similar to natural guanine, but only retains binding activity and has no significant antibacterial function, while modified purines are typically investigated as nucleic acid metabolism inhibitors and this compound could potentially act as an antiviral or anticancer agent by inhibiting essential enzymes in DNA/RNA synthesis.

RNA information: TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, but contains the UCU bulge structure that binds to HIV-1 Tat. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as SPR. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and assists in distinguishing non-specific binding from specific interactions.; TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, but contains the UCU bulge structure that binds to HIV-2 Tat. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as SPR. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and assists in distinguishing non-specific binding from specific interactions.; TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, but contains the UCU bulge structure that binds to HIV-3 Tat. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as SPR. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and assists in distinguishing non-specific binding from specific interactions.; TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, but contains the UCU bulge structure that binds to HIV-4 Tat. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as SPR. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and assists in distinguishing non-specific binding from specific interactions.; TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, but contains the UCU bulge structure that binds to HIV-5 Tat. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as SPR. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and assists in distinguishing non-specific binding from specific interactions.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-(2-aminoethyl)-5-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]-2-phenylmethoxyphenyl]ethanamine, also referred to as Compound 5a (a 3,5,2,6-tetrakis(2-aminoethyl) p-terphenyl compound with a single methoxy group at the 2 position of the central benzene ring), is a derivative of 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]phenol, where the phenol is substituted with a benzyloxy group, and Compound 5a is synthesized via sequential palladium-catalyzed Suzuki coupling reactions and was characterized by methods such as Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) and Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR); the benzyloxy group is an aromatic side chain that increases lipophilicity, and Compound 5a functionally can bind to the hairpin RNA (Bh) of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, has limited selectivity for Bh over DNA, exhibits a high half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC) in inhibiting the interaction between Bh and Rev, and shows no antiviral activity in cellular assays; as a tetracationic polyamine, its primary mode of action remains strong interaction with negatively charged biomolecules (nucleic acids, membranes), and in practical applications, this benzyloxy-substituted polyamine is often used in Structure-Activity Relationship (SAR) studies to evaluate the effect of simple aromatic lipophilic side chains on the polyamine cores cytotoxicity, transfection efficiency, and selectivity, to find the optimal drug design.

RNA information: STPY-xpt is a guanine-sensing riboswitch. Its structural features include a complex secondary structure composed of three stem rings (P1, P2, P3), which are organized around a tridirectional junction point to form guanine binding sites. Functionally, STPY-xpt can specifically recognize and bind to guanine. This binding event triggers the structural rearrangement of the riboswitch, thereby regulating the expression of downstream genes. Specifically, after binding to guanine, STPY-xpt tends to form a terminating substructure, leading to premature transcription termination. Furthermore, STPY-xpt demonstrated a relatively high 2AP (2-aminopine) binding affinity in the experiment, which is consistent with its highly efficient transcriptional regulatory efficiency in vivo. This high affinity may enable STPY-xpt to effectively regulate gene expression even at a lower concentration of guanine, thereby playing a key role in the metabolic regulation of bacteria.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-6-methyl-N-[3-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl]thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as a promising antiviral candidate, is an amide compound containing thienopyridine and trifluoromethylaniline structures whose structure is based on the thienopyridine parent skeleton: a thiophene ring is fused with a pyridine ring, substituted with an amino group at C3, a methyl group at C6, and a 3-(trifluoromethyl)phenylcarboxamide group at C2, which selectively targets the HIV TAR RNA, a crucial structured region within the viral 5 UTR, with its binding primarily driven by van der Waals forces, supplemented by hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions, an interaction that disrupts a specific structural element demonstrating high selectivity despite the complexity of the 365-nucleotide UTR sequence, and in terms of biological function, thienopyridine derivatives are often investigated as Forkhead Box M1 (FOXM1) inhibitors (potential anticancer agents), as well as for their anti-inflammatory, antidepressant, and antimicrobial activities, while this compound itself exhibits a lack of toxicity and possesses good antiviral activity, and these combined propertiesits effective mechanism, selectivity, and safety profilemake it a highly attractive candidate for further therapeutic development.

RNA information: No loop (loopless RNA), also known as 5'CAG/3'CUG and 5'CAG/3'GUC, is an RNA with a hairpin structure. Structurally, its stem has fully complementary base pairs (no mismatches/deletions) and no internal loops, showing high conformational stability without inter-strand "breathing effect" as a simplified dsRNA model. Functionally, it serves as a key experimental control to distinguish small molecules’ specific/non-specific binding to RNA, with no direct link to specific diseases.
Small_molecule_information: (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one, also referred to as Piperine (a major bioactive alkaloid and phytochemical compound derived from black pepper (Piper nigrum) that is responsible for the pungent taste of pepper and has key characteristics including low water solubility and poor bioavailability), is a compound that is either a natural product derivative or a structural analogue, typically categorized with compounds related to Piperine. Its structure is characterized by a 1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl group (a signature motif derived from Piperine and common in natural products), linked via a conjugated diene bridge (composed of two double bonds) to a piperidin-1-yl-ketone (amide) moiety. This structure is highly planar and possesses an extended conjugated system, often resulting in a colored solid with potential unique fluorescence properties. Piperine and its derivatives exhibit various pharmacological activities, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, analgesic effects, and are most renowned for their bioavailability enhancing properties by inhibiting certain metabolizing enzymes like CYP3A4, with Piperine itself pharmacologically renowned for its enhancer effect, significantly increasing the absorption and efficacy of various drugs and nutrients by inhibiting digestive enzymes and drug-metabolizing enzymes like cytochrome P450, and also possessing intrinsic anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and potential neuroprotective properties; the specific piperidine substitution in (2E,4E)-5-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-1-piperidin-1-ylpenta-2,4-dien-1-one increases its lipophilicity, facilitating its passage across cell membranes, and it is often studied as a potential active component of natural product-based drugs or as an adjuvant to enhance the absorption of other pharmaceuticals.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Kaempferol, is a natural flavonol compound structurally similar to Quercetin (2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one) and Myricetin (3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), differing mainly in the number of hydroxyl groups on the B ring, having only one at position 4, with the formula C15H10O6. Kaempferol is found in a variety of plant foods, such as tea, broccoli, grapefruit, and herbs. Functionally, as a dietary flavonol, Kaempferol possesses significant antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and potential anticancer properties, it modulates cell signaling pathways (e.g., PI3K/Akt) and is studied for cardiovascular and neurodegenerative disease prevention, research also suggests it can regulate apoptosis, inhibit angiogenesis, and suppress cell proliferation, thereby potentially inhibiting tumor development, its role in the prevention of chronic diseases and the maintenance of cardiovascular health is particularly studied, although its bioactivity may differ from other flavonols due to the single hydroxyl group on its B ring.

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[2-(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-2-yl)ethenyl]-4-nitrophenol, also referred to as Compound 5, is a green-yellow 1-methylquinolinium derivative with a 2-hydroxy-5-nitrostyryl group at the quinoline 2-position and no substitution at the 4-position, and this cationic dye links 1-methylquinolin-1-ium with 4-nitrophenol via an ethenyl bridge; its SPR assay shows it has lower affinity for A10-RNA-Wt than analogs with methylpiperazine, its dual-luciferase assay reveals it slightly inhibits ADAM10 translation, and it has cell toxicity with relevant cell lines showing certain toxicity characteristics, meanwhile its strong push-pull electronic system confers high photophysical properties and potential solvatochromism, the phenolic hydroxyl group exhibits pH sensitivity upon deprotonation, therefore this compound is used as a pH-responsive fluorescent probe for monitoring pH changes in cellular or tissue microenvironments.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[2-[[2-[(5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl)amino]acetyl]amino]acetyl]amino]-3-phenylpropanoic acid. This is a tripeptide (triglycine)-heterocycle conjugate containing a phenylalanine residue, linked to the cationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium scaffold. This strategy of coupling a cytotoxic aromatic heterocycle with natural amino acids or peptide segments is used in medicinal chemistry to improve the compounds bioavailability and targeting. The practical application of this compound is being explored in prodrug development. Its structure may be designed to be hydrolyzed by specific enzymes (such as proteases highly expressed in tumor cells), selectively releasing the cytotoxic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core at the tumor site, thereby achieving tumor-selective treatment.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 24-methyl-5,7,18,20-tetraoxa-24-azoniahexacyclo[11.11.0.02,10.04,8.014,22.017,21]tetracosa-1(24),2,4(8),9,11,13,15,17(21),22-nonaene, also referred to as Sanguinarine, is a highly complex, polycyclic cationic compound, which can be chemically identified as the N-methyl derivative of a macrocyclic tetraoxaza-heteroatom system, and its molecular structure is dominated by its extensive ring fusion as indicated by its IUPAC name, and Sanguinarine is nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that can specifically bind to the binding pocket of Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA, inducing significant structural changes in the RNA, Sanguinarine is also a benzophenanthridine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and pro-apoptotic effects, it targets multiple cellular pathways, including NF-B, and is studied for anticancer applications despite potential toxicity concerns, the compounds formula, C21H16NO4, reveals a structure containing 21 carbon atoms, 4 oxygen atoms, and a single nitrogen atom that carries a permanent positive charge (azonia) due to methylation, the core hexacyclic structure suggests a rigid, cage-like framework featuring two major 12-membered rings bridged by various fused smaller rings (implied by the [11.11.0.02,10.04,8.014,22.017,21] nomenclature), leading to a highly planar and stiff molecular geometry, the presence of four oxygen atoms and nine double bonds (nonaene) indicates extensive unsaturation, and the presence of oxygen atoms at positions 5, 7, 18, and 20 suggests they are likely incorporated into ether or furan-like structures, strongly suggesting this molecule is an analog of macrocyclic dyes or natural products with rigid, fluorescent properties, potentially related to porphyrins or cryptands in shape, although Sanguinarine has a high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA, it fails to activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, possibly due to its toxicity to cells, nevertheless, Sanguinarine, as one of the potential ligands, provides important clues for studying the functional mechanism of this riboswitch, due to its fixed positive charge and large, planar aromatic system, this compound is highly likely to function as an intercalating agent for nucleic acids, making it a potential DNA/RNA binding fluorescent probe for biological imaging, or a promising scaffold for anti-cancer or antimicrobial drug development, where its rigidity could confer high target specificity.

RNA information: HCV IRES subdomain IIa is an evolutionarily conserved RNA structural element that can specifically bind to benzimidazole-class small molecules. It controls the homeostasis of viral protein synthesis by modulating the conformation of the internal ribosome entry site, thereby regulating cap-independent translation initiation.
Small_molecule_information: 1-[15-[(dimethylamino)methyl]-6-oxa-11,13,17-triazatetracyclo[8.7.0.02,7.012,17]heptadeca-1(10),2(7),8,11-tetraen-4-yl]-N,N-dimethylmethanamine, also referred to as Benzimidazole 13 (Isis-13), is a complex polycyclic macrocyclic structure with multiple heteroatoms, and Benzimidazole 13 (Isis-13) is a bicyclic small molecule purposely optimized to recognize and lock the hepatitis C virus (HCV) internal ribosome entry site (IRES) subdomain IIa, an RNA stem-bulge element that must maintain its native 90 bend for efficient cap-independent viral translation, built on a 2-aminobenzimidazole core, Isis-13 simultaneously constrains both the N1 and C6 side-chains into cyclic motifsN1 into a pyrrolidine and C6 into a tetrahydropyranyielding a significant affinity gain over the original hit while remaining non-toxic to human hepatoma cells, due to its unique scaffold, it is a potential candidate for novel antimicrobial agents or receptor antagonists, mimicking biological active sites, in the binding pocket the two protonatable dimethylamino groups replace two divalent-metal hot spots detected in crystal structures; the planar benzimidazole ring -stacks with G5, forcing the bulge to straighten by 23 and thereby mis-align the downstream IIb hairpin, this conformational switch blocks eIF2-GDP release, stalls 80-S ribosome assembly and suppresses HCV replication in genotype-1b replicon and infectious JFH-1 luciferase-reporter assays with notable potency, a potency that tracks quantitatively with its RNA-binding constant and is retained against common clinical IIa mutants, the favorable in-vitro window, confirmed mechanism of action and synthetic scalability identify Isis-13 as a validated lead for structure-guided development of next-generation RNA-directed antivirals against hepatitis C.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 1-ethyl-3-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-7-[(3S)-3-methylpiperazin-4-ium-1-yl]quinolin-2-one, also referred to as SMSM48, is a complex polyheterocyclic structure centered on a quinolin-2-one core (a non-planar coumarin analog with a 4-methylpiperazine A-ring and imidazopyridine DE-fused system), substituted at the 3-position with an imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine ring and at the 7-position with a chiral, permanently positively charged piperazin-4-ium cation (carrying a 1 charge), which can recognize the single-G bulge of RNA1 driven by minor-groove H-bonds, -stacking and electrostatics (confirmed by FP and SAR comparison) and serves as a selective RNA ligand; the quinolin-2-one scaffold is common in central nervous system (CNS) active drugs, while the imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine is often found in kinase inhibitors, and the permanent positive charge and chirality are key features influencing its biological distribution and target binding, in reality, this compound would be investigated as a potential tyrosine kinase inhibitor or a GPCR modulator for conditions such as cancer, inflammation, or neurodegenerative disorders.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4-methylbenzenesulfonate;(2Z)-3-methyl-2-[(1-methylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)methylidene]-1,3-benzothiazole, also referred to as Thiazole Orange, is an organic salt containing quinolinium and benzothiazole heterocyclic rings that intercalates into DNA and RNA, commonly used in flow cytometry and molecular biology for detecting nucleic acids; its structure is based on the quinoline and benzothiazole parent skeletons: a benzene ring is fused with a pyridine ring to form the quinoline skeleton, and a benzene ring is fused with a thiazole ring to form the benzothiazole skeleton, with these two parent nuclei connected by a methine group. In terms of biological function, this class of compounds typically functions as dyes, photosensitizers, or exhibits specific biological enzyme inhibitory activities (Benzothiazoles are important in therapeutic chemistry).

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N-[7-[3-(dimethylamino)propoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]-N,N-dimethylpropane-1,3-diamine, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine or quaternary ammonium salt side chains. This structural class is known for DNA/RNA binding and topoisomerase inhibition, with many such compounds developed as broad-spectrum anticancer agents. Real-world applications may involve their use as novel anti-tumor leads or fluorescent probes for cancer therapy or biological research.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: [(2R,3S,4R,5R)-5-(3-carbamoylpyridin-1-ium-1-yl)-3,4-dihydroxyoxolan-2-yl]methyl hydrogen phosphate, also referred to as Nicotinamide mononucleotide (NMN), is the phosphorylated form of Nicotinamide Mononucleotide (NMN), where the nicotinamide group carries a positive charge, and it is a non-azaaromatic nucleotide derivative with a structure consisting of a nicotinamide ring, ribose moiety, and phosphate group that lacks planar azaaromatic features, binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate its-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis; NMN is a direct precursor of NAD and has garnered significant attention in anti-aging and mitochondrial function research, with its core role being acting as a direct precursor for synthesizing NAD (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide) to support cellular energy metabolism (unrelated to azaaromatic sensing), and the molecule indirectly regulates various metabolic and repair processes by elevating cellular NAD levels and is considered a potential longevity molecule or neuroprotective agent.

Small_molecule_information: 7-methoxy-1-methyl-4,9-dihydro-3H-pyrido[3,4-b]indole, also referred to as harmaline, is a tetrahydro--carboline (THBC) derivative which is a planar azaaromatic -carboline alkaloid with a fused tricyclic core (indole and pyridine rings, containing multiple nitrogen atoms) and a methoxy group at the 7-position, plus a hydrogenated double bond in the pyridine ring (distinct from harmine), and THBCs are often involved in neurotransmitter modulation in the brain, with this compound binding to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) via non-specific interactions but with lower affinity than chelerythrine, failing to activate riboswitch-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis while having monoamine oxidase inhibitory activity which aids in studying riboswitch ligand structure-activity relationships, and THBCs are explored for their neuroprotective and antioxidant properties, with this molecule being a candidate for research as a ligand for 5-HT (serotonin) or dopamine receptors in the context of neurological and psychiatric drug development.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-3-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-N-(2-pyrrolidin-1-ylethyl)-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine iodide, also referred to as the N5-methylated analogue of 4b-2, is the quaternary ammonium salt based on the benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline core. It is designed as a potent cytotoxic agent, likely acting as a DNA intercalator, and is investigated for its structure-activity relationship in antitumor drug development; this derivative is one of the strongest binders in its series for the NRQ, exhibits strong anti-proliferative activity in A375, yet has poor selectivity due to its binding to mutant and hairpin oligomers.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE IIBh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide derived from the subdomain IIB of the HIV-1 Rev Response Element (RRE). Its core function is to serve as a high-affinity binding site for the viral Rev protein, particularly its arginine-rich α-helical region (Rev₃₄₋₅₀). This interaction is essential for triggering the nuclear export of unspliced or incompletely spliced viral RNA, thereby ensuring the production of viral particles during the late stage of the HIV-1 life cycle.
Small_molecule_information: [3-(3,6-dichlorocarbazol-9-yl)-2-hydroxypropyl]-(2-hydroxyethyl)-methylazanium, also referred to as a dichlorocarbazole, is a compound containing a carbazole core and a single quaternary ammonium salt side chain, with chloro substitutions at positions 3 and 6 of the carbazole, with the formula C18H21Cl2N2O2 (cationic part), this molecule has moderate binding affinity but poor specificity, shows antiviral effects but likely through non-specific nucleic acid interactions rather than targeted Rev inhibition, and it combines a lipophilic dichlorocarbazole core with a hydrophilic quaternary ammonium head; chlorinated carbazole derivatives often exhibit cytotoxicity and antiviral activity, while the quaternary ammonium structure is often designed to enhance water solubility and cell membrane penetration, leading to its investigation as a cationic surfactant or novel bactericide with broad-spectrum antimicrobial or antifungal activity by disrupting microbial cell membranes.

RNA information: The r(CGG)12 sequence is a CGG-repeat RNA motif derived from the FMR1 5′UTR that functions as a protein-binding ribo-regulator. In cells it folds into a hairpin displaying multiple 5′CGG/3′GGC internal loops; these loops act as tandem recognition sites for RNA-processing factors such as DGCR8, Sam68 and hnRNP A2/B1. By sequestering these proteins into nuclear foci, r(CGG)12 perturbs alternative pre-mRNA splicing and, in the absence of an AUG start codon, it also templates repeat-associated non-ATG (RAN) translation to produce toxic poly-glycine-containing peptides. Thus, r(CGG)12 behaves like a disease-linked riboswitch-like element whose “ligand” is the protein machinery it binds, and its homeostatic imbalance drives the molecular pathology of fragile-X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS).
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[3-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 2 (Ht-N3, a bis-benzimidazole derivative with Hoechst 33258 core, 2-(piperidin-1-yl)ethoxy side chain, azide group, and 4-fluorophenylethyl tail replacing FMNs ribose-phosphate), is an azide-containing bis-benzimidazole probe without the tert-butyl groups (compared to N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide), its core activity remains the same: strong DNA binding, and it additionally binds 5CGG/3GGC and pre-miR-210, disrupts r(CGG)-DGCR8, restores SMN2/Bcl-x splicing, inhibits tumor growth, has low cytotoxicity while tRNA reduces its selectivity, serving as a probe/lead for FXTAS, CGG-repeat disorders, and miR-210-driven cancers; its real-world activity is as a Click Chemistry probe for nucleic acid binding studies and labeling, which enables researchers to map the interaction site of the bis-benzimidazole unit in a biological context using the high specificity of the azide group.

Small_molecule_information: 7H-purine, also referred to as a planar hydrophobic bicyclic azaaromatic compound that interacts non-specifically with the yjdF motif RNA and shows minimal activation of riboswitch-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, is a bicyclic nitrogen-containing aromatic system formed by the fusion of a pyrimidine ring and an imidazole ring with the molecular formula CHN, and it represents one of the most essential structural frameworks in living organisms as the backbone of DNA and RNA bases such as adenine and guanine and of key coenzymes including ATP and NADH, with purine derivatives playing crucial roles in genetic information transfer, energy metabolism, and signal transduction and serving widely in medicinal chemistry as the basis for anticancer, antiviral, and immunosuppressive agents.

RNA information: NRQ mutant is a G-base mutant of NRQ that prevents the formation of the G4 structure.
Small_molecule_information: 5-methyl-N-[2-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)ethyl]-3-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-10H-indolo[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-amine iodide, also referred to as the N5-methylated analogue of 4d-1 (indoloquinoline with 3-styryl group), is a highly potent indolo[3,2-b]quinoline quaternary ammonium salt featuring an N-methylpiperazine side chain, which exhibited affinity for the NRQ and good anti-proliferative activity in A375 cells, and this structure combines the high DNA affinity of the cationic core with the optimized properties provided by the piperazine, making it a lead compound in anticancer research.

RNA information: TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, but contains the UCU bulge structure that binds to HIV-1 Tat. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as SPR. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and assists in distinguishing non-specific binding from specific interactions.; TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control.
