Small_molecule_information: 3-[[(2S)-2-[[(2R)-1-[(E,2R,7R)-8-[(2R)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-azaniumylpropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-2,7-bis[(2-ethylquinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]-8-oxooct-4-enoyl]pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]-3-phenylpropanoyl]amino]propylazanium, also referred to as Compound 3E, is a complex peptidomimetic that is an olefin-linked analog derived from an earlier disulfide-bridged lead, retains two heteroaromatic intercalating units essential for binding the HIV-1 FSS RNA upper stem-loop, has moderate binding affinity and shows sequence selectivity over nonspecific RNAs but did not demonstrate antiviral activity in pseudotyped HIV assays which motivated the structural optimization that led to incorporation of larger benzo[g]quinoline groups in later analogs, and this compound differs from 3-[[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-[(E,2S,7S)-8-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-azaniumylpropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]carbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-2,7-bis[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)amino]-8-oxooct-4-enoyl]pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]-3-phenylpropanoyl]amino]propylazanium in two major ways: the core and proline residues are of non-natural (2R,7R) and (2R) chirality, making it an enantiomer or diastereomer of related compounds; and the highly hydrophobic 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl group is simplified to the smaller, less lipophilic 2-ethylquinoline-3-carbonyl group, the inversion of chirality means that this compound is likely to exhibit completely different biological activities and potentially higher aqueous solubility due to the smaller aromatic group, and it may be designed to target receptors or enzymes that are inaccessible or poorly bound by the S-enantiomer.

RNA information: The bacterial riboflavin (FMN) riboswitch is located in the 5' untranslated region (5' UTR) of mRNA and consists of an aptamer domain and an expression platform. It can specifically bind to FMN and regulate the expression of the downstream key riboflavin synthesis gene ribB through conformational changes. When FMN is sufficient, the riboswitch forms a terminator/sequestering loop to inhibit gene expression; when FMN is deficient, it switches to an antiterminator/anti-sequestering loop, allowing gene transcription and translation to achieve negative feedback regulation. This riboswitch exists exclusively in bacteria and has no homologous structure in mammals, making it a highly selective target for new antibacterial drugs (such as ribocil).
Small_molecule_information: [5-(7,8-dimethyl-2,4-dioxobenzo[g]pteridin-10-yl)-2,3,4-trihydroxypentyl] dihydrogen phosphate, also referred to as Flavin Mononucleotide (FMN), is a derivative of riboflavin generated through phosphorylation, which is a coenzyme produced by the phosphorylation of riboflavin; its isoalloxazine ring has a planar aromatic structure, while the phosphate group is flexibly linked to the ring system, and its isoalloxazine tricyclic and ribitol-phosphate skeleton together form a reversible electron-transferring redox center; as the natural ligand of bacterial riboswitches, FMN inserts into the aptamer pocket and induces conformational changes in RNA, causing the expression platform to form a terminator/sequestering loop, and it can also induce RNA structure folding into terminator clips or blocking ribosome binding sites by binding to the SA1 riboswitch upstream of riboflavin synthesis genes and the SA2 riboswitch upstream of transport genes, using its own concentration changes as a signal, which consequently shuts down the transcription and translation of the downstream riboflavin synthesis gene ribB as well as the de novo synthesis and uptake pathways of riboflavin, thereby achieving negative feedback regulation of riboflavin biosynthesis; because it is crucial for bacterial survival and does not exist in mammals, FMN and its riboswitch have become targets for antibiotic design, which can be competitionally occupied by structural analogues such as rosettin and ribocil-C, blocking the supply of riboflavin and forcing bacteria to starve to death.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5-dichloro-4-phenylmethoxybenzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Compound P7, is a compound based on the benzamidine scaffold, substituted with two chlorine atoms and a benzyloxy group on the benzene ring, with the formula C14H12Cl2N2O; it is a dichloro-substituted mono-imidamide derivative, cationic due to the imidamide group, retains the twin benzamidine cations of pentamidine but is elongated with a nine-carbon methylene linker that maximizes its shape complementarity to the major groove of the 5CUG/3GUC hairpin repeat, the benzyloxy group enhances lipophilicity and steric bulk, is a pentamidine-derived small molecule optimized through 3-D shape-based similarity screening as a next-generation ligand for the toxic r(CUG)exp RNA that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), can dissociate MBNL1 from r(CUG)12 in a TR-FRET displacement assay and partially shifts a bichromatic MBNL1 exon 5 reporter toward the normal exon-skipped DsRed signal in DM1 cell models though cytotoxicity becomes evident at higher doses, is less selective than P1 as it binds with similar affinity to RNAs containing 5CCG/3GUC or 5CAG/3GCC motifs and to the human A-site, and such compounds are researched as novel antibacterial and antiparasitic agents, with activity often attributed to the specific binding and electrostatic interaction of the cationic structure with the negatively charged DNA of the microorganism, while Compound P7 itself exemplifies how computational shape similarity can rapidly evolve an antimicrobial template into a low-micromolar chemical probe for DM1 RNA that may serve as a stepping-stone toward in-vivo-compatible DM1 therapeutics.

It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: (E,2R,11R)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,11-bis[[(2S)-2-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)-propylamino]propanoyl]amino]dodec-6-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 11 (2012), is a giant, symmetrical peptidomimetic dimer that is closely related to compound 10 but differs in linker geometry, producing subtle variations in conformational dynamics which influences how the benzo[g]quinoline elements align with repeat motifs and contributes to a broader interaction profile, and structurally this highlights the importance of optimizing linker design to balance stability, affinity, and selectivity while functionally it shows significant bioactivity not only in cell assays but also in a DM1 mouse model, where it partially restores proper splicing patterns, making it a key proof-of-concept that synthetic small molecules can achieve therapeutic rescue of RNA-mediated disease in vivo; its backbone is a twelve-carbon chain containing a trans (E) double bond, giving it a relatively extended geometric conformation, and the molecule possesses significant polycationic character due to the presence of multiple amine groups, including the 6-amino groups of two Lysine side chains and two 3-aminopropylamine termini, which polycationic nature is key to designing highly efficient DNA minor groove binders, as it enables potent electrostatic binding to the negatively charged DNA phosphate backbone, furthermore, benzo[g]quinoline groups are attached to both sides, providing DNA intercalation potential or acting as fluorescent reporters, and this design, integrating dual heads, polycationicity, and DNA intercalation units, is a strategy for creating a dual-mechanism antitumor drug, likely acting via topoisomerase inhibition and/or DNA damage.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 4-(1H-benzimidazol-2-yl)benzenecarbothioamide, also referred to as 2-(4-Thiocarbamoylphenyl)benzimidazole, is a combination of benzimidazole and carbothioamide structures, and 2-(4-Thiocarbamoylphenyl)benzimidazole is a heterocyclic small molecule with a benzimidazole core linked to a thiocarbamoyl-substituted phenyl group, the benzimidazole core is often associated with antiparasitic, antifungal, antiviral, and antineoplastic activities, the carbothioamide group may enable the molecule to act as a metal chelator or enzyme inhibitor, the structure suggests the molecule may serve as a broad-spectrum antimicrobial agent, acting on cell division or specific enzyme systems, unlike myricetin, it lacks reported affinity for CAG-repeat RNAs in HD/SCAs but may interact with structured RNAs via benzimidazole intercalation and thiocarbamoyl H-bonding, it shows no validated activity against polyQ aggregation or mitochondrial dysfunction in HD models, though its benzimidazole motif could potentially target nucleic acid mismatches, making it a speculative scaffold for TRED-focused optimization with further biophysical validation needed. -

RNA information: RNA2 is defined by a 1x1 nucleotide GG internal loop that is adjacent to the Drosha site in the pri-miR-96 structure. It is the specific and high-affinity binding target for compound 2, with a Kd of 1,500 nM, but does not bind compound 1.
Small_molecule_information: N-(3-azidopropyl)-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 1, is a mono-benzimidazole Click Chemistry probe with tert-butyl and azide groups, which is a benzimidazole-based RNA-binding module previously identified by the Informa platform to avidly bind a 1x1 nucleotide UU internal loop within the Drosha processing site of the pri-miR-96 hairpin precursor, inhibits miR-96 biogenesis and induces apoptosis in breast cancer cells with activity that limits its in vivo utility, shows no detectable binding to DNA, and its real-world activity is dedicated to biological orthogonal labeling experiments for target validation and mechanism elucidation.

RNA information: The duplex sequence is a designed RNA composed of complementary CUG and CAG repeats that form a stable double helix. Structurally, it lacks the mismatches present in expanded repeats, instead adopting a continuous A-form conformation that eliminates the irregular binding pockets exploited by proteins or ligands in single-stranded hairpins. Functionally, the duplex acts as a control to demonstrate whether ligands bind specifically to mismatched hairpin structures or indiscriminately to generic RNA helices. Its inclusion highlights the importance of structural context in ligand recognition and helps identify molecules with true selectivity for pathogenic RNA conformations.
Small_molecule_information: (Z,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 42012, is the cis (Z) isomer of (E,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, a benzo[g]quinoline-containing Z-isomer designed through olefin metathesis, with the double bond in the core oct-4-enediamide scaffold having the cis configuration, and its geometry places the heteroaromatic moiety in an orientation that promotes favorable stacking interactions with RNA bases, compared to earlier disulfide-linked scaffolds, the olefin bioisostere of this compound improves chemical stability and compatibility for cellular studies, structurally, it exemplifies the importance of precise spatial placement of aromatic and peptide elements to achieve cooperative binding across repeat sequences, functionally, it enters cells efficiently and demonstrates biological activity by alleviating RNA-mediated toxicity in model systems, making it a strong lead structure for RNA-targeted small-molecule drug design, this conformational difference introduces a fixed bend in the molecules three-dimensional structure, in complex peptidomimetics, such cis-trans isomerization is often the decisive factor for biological activity, as it affects the precise spatial matching with the target binding pocket, the compound maintains the poly-cationic and highly aromatic features of its counterpart, its potential applications are similar to those of peptidomimetics, but the activity and selectivity profile of (Z,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide will be significantly altered due to the cis conformation, making it a critical tool for studying the impact of molecular conformation on biological activity.

RNA information: (5'CNG/3'CNG) denotes repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide) predominantly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures via specific base pairing. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing and drive disease.
Small_molecule_information: 8-(9-carboxy-4-formyl-1,3,6-trihydroxyphenazin-2-yl)-6-formyl-4,7,9-trihydroxyphenazine-1-carboxylic acid, also referred to as Dilomofungin, is an extremely complex dimeric molecule, consisting of two highly substituted phenazine units linked by a carbon-carbon bond, which is a spontaneous dimer of lomofungin formed in DMSO and structurally features a carboncarbon bond between two lomofungin monomers. The phenazine scaffold is a heterocyclic system made of two benzene rings fused with two nitrogen atoms, and many natural phenazine derivatives (like pyocyanin) are pigments produced by microorganisms with potent antibiotic and biological redox activities. This compound possesses multiple functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and formyl groups, which lend the molecule multiple hydrogen bond donor/acceptor capabilities and high water solubility, and it exhibits higher potency than lomofungin in inhibiting MBNL1-CUG RNA binding in vitro, while also having strong binding affinity and the ability to partially rescue mis-splicing in DM1 cell models, though it has the unintended effect of stabilizing toxic CUG repeat RNA, leading to its accumulation in nuclear foci and reduced transcript turnover, which limits its therapeutic potential for myotonic dystrophy type 1. The phenazine rings establish it as a potent redox-active molecule capable of mediating electron transfer in biological systems. In reality, such structurally complex phenazine derivatives are often explored as novel antibiotics or anticancer agents, where the mechanism of action might involve DNA intercalation or the generation of reactive oxygen species to induce apoptosis. This molecule could also serve as a chelating agent or redox indicator in biochemical analysis.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2,3,9,10-tetramethoxy-5,6-dihydroisoquinolino[2,1-b]isoquinolin-7-ium;chloride;hydrate, also referred to as Palmatine Chloride Hydrate, is a protoberberine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and potential anticancer effects that interacts with DNA/RNA and modulates signaling pathways, and this molecule is a structural analog of the well-known natural product Berberine, belonging to the protoberberine class of alkaloids, characterized by its tetramethoxy substitution and quaternary ammonium structure; its natural origin and broad bioactivity make it a candidate for drug development, and the real-world pharmacological activities of this class are extensive: Berberine is widely used for its natural anti-diabetic, lipid-lowering, and broad-spectrum antibacterial effects, its planar cationic structure allows it to efficiently intercalate DNA and disrupt microbial replication, consequently, this compound holds significant therapeutic potential in the management of metabolic syndrome (diabetes, hyperlipidemia) and infectious diseases, often studied as an AMPK activator to regulate energy metabolism.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: bis(2-acetamidoacetic acid);4-[2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)iminohydrazinyl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Diminazine, is a complex salt molecule consisting of a large cationic formamidine hydrazonebased diamidine species and two 2-acetamidoacetic acid anions, and the ,-diamidine core connected through a biphenyl and hydrazone bridge corresponds to the class of aromatic diamidine compounds used as antiprotozoal agents that exert activity by binding to the minor groove of DNA, with such cationic diamidines commonly displaying antiprotozoal, antifungal, antiviral, anticancer, or broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties.

RNA information: r(CUG)109 is a pathogenic RNA motif composed of 109 CUG repeats that folds into a stable, elongated hairpin structure containing periodic 1×1 UU internal loops. It functions as a toxic RNA by specifically binding to and sequestering muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, thereby disrupting its normal splicing regulation and contributing to the molecular pathology of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Small molecules that selectively bind r(CUG)109 can displace MBNL1, restore aberrant splicing patterns, and alleviate disease-associated phenotypes in cellular models .
Small_molecule_information: N-[4-[6-(furan-2-carbonylamino)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenyl]furan-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Benzimidazole 16 (2015), is a complex aromatic heterocyclic compound centered around a benzimidazole core, a planar heterocycle formed by the fusion of a benzene and an imidazole ring, and Benzimidazole 16 (2015) is a 2-phenyl-benzimidazole-4-carboxamide derivative engineered to selectively bind the toxic r(CUG)109 RNA expansion that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1, the benzimidazole is substituted at position 2 with a 4-aminophenyl-furan-2-carboxamide side chain, and at position 6 with a furan-2-carbonylamino side chain, the entire molecule is composed of multiple planar aromatic rings (benzimidazole, phenyl, and two furan rings) linked by amide bonds, creating an extended, rigid, and highly conjugated system, the amide and benzimidazole moieties provide multiple hydrogen bond donors and acceptors, this scaffold is often designed as a protein kinase inhibitor or receptor ligand, benzimidazole compounds are a privileged scaffold in medicinal chemistry due to their diverse biological activities, frequently exhibiting antiparasitic, antiviral, and anticancer properties, the activity of this specific compound is likely related to its potential as a molecular probe or as a lead compound in the search for new drugs, by mimicking the recognition surface of MBNL1, it engages the periodic UU internal loops of the CUG hairpin with notable potency in TR-FRET displacement, displaces MBNL1 from ribonuclear foci, and restores correct alternative splicing of the cTNT mini-gene in DM1 cell models without altering DMPK mRNA abundance or affecting off-target RNAs such as human A-site rRNA or r(GGCC)4, its drug-like physicochemical profilemoderate molecular weight (352 Da), cLogP 3.9, topological polar surface area 111 , and negligible cytotoxicity up to 100 Mdemonstrates that uncharged, benzimidazole-based small molecules can selectively drug structured, repetitive RNA motifs and rescue disease-relevant splicing defects. RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 1-[3-[(17-methoxy-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-yl)oxy]propyl]-N,N-dimethylpyridin-1-ium-4-amine is a dicationic structure, where one cation is the core pentacyclic scaffold, and the other is 4-(dimethylamino)pyridinium, a positively charged derivative of DMAP. The 4-(dimethylamino)pyridinium structure has relatively high electron density and hydrophilicity, adding complexity to the molecule. Functionally, this combination of a large lipophilic scaffold and a basic quaternary ammonium salt is often explored as a mitochondria-targeting agent, utilizing the high negative potential across the mitochondrial membrane for accumulation within the organelle. Potential applications include therapeutic research for diseases involving mitochondrial dysfunction, such as certain cancers or neurodegenerative disorders.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 2,3,9,10-tetramethoxy-5,6-dihydroisoquinolino[2,1-b]isoquinolin-7-ium;chloride;hydrate, also referred to as Palmatine Chloride Hydrate, is a protoberberine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and potential anticancer effects that interacts with DNA/RNA and modulates signaling pathways, and this molecule is a structural analog of the well-known natural product Berberine, belonging to the protoberberine class of alkaloids, characterized by its tetramethoxy substitution and quaternary ammonium structure; its natural origin and broad bioactivity make it a candidate for drug development, and the real-world pharmacological activities of this class are extensive: Berberine is widely used for its natural anti-diabetic, lipid-lowering, and broad-spectrum antibacterial effects, its planar cationic structure allows it to efficiently intercalate DNA and disrupt microbial replication, consequently, this compound holds significant therapeutic potential in the management of metabolic syndrome (diabetes, hyperlipidemia) and infectious diseases, often studied as an AMPK activator to regulate energy metabolism.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: N,N-dimethyl-N-[2-[(E)-2-(4-methylsulfanylphenyl)ethenyl]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]ethane-1,2-diamine, also referred to as a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with a p-(methylthio)styryl group at the 2-position and an N1,N1-dimethylethane-1,2-diamine side chain at the 11-position, has the N,N-dimethylethane-1,2-diamine side chain, a short-chain dimethyl tertiary amine derivative; this compound exhibited moderate binding affinity to the NRAS RNA G-quadruplex (NRQ) and showed potent anti-proliferative activity across all tested cancer cell lines including notable activity in A375 melanoma cells, and the strong basicity of the tertiary amine terminus results in a positively charged species at physiological pH, promoting strong electrostatic interactions and accumulation in acidic organelles, which makes it a potent DNA binding agent and a lead compound for lysosome-targeting anticancer drugs.

RNA information: pre-miR-21 is a stem-loop non-coding RNA precursor with a distinct structure, containing G28-A29 (Dicer-TRBP cleavage site) in the stem and single-stranded bulges/apical loops (small-molecule binding pockets). It processes into oncogenic mature miR-21; ligands like 4a or bPGN  bind specifically, inhibiting Dicer processing, upregulating tumor suppressors (PDCD4, PTEN), and suppressing cancer cell proliferation. It is a druggable target for cancers such as colorectal cancer.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(2,2-dimethyl-1,3-dihydropyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridin-2-ium-6-yl)methyl]-3-(trifluoromethyl)aniline, also referred to as 4n (a derivative of 2-amino-dihydropyrrolopyridine), is a permanently cationic heterocyclic compound, with 4n having a methylated pyrrolidine ring that modifies its original HBD/HBA and being synthesized via modular routes including Ru-catalyzed [222] cycloaddition and Boc deprotection, which binds pre-miR-21, and unlike analogs like 4a, its pyrrolidine methylation abrogates Dicer inhibition activity, showing structure-activity links critical for optimizing pre-miR-21-targeted anticancer ligands; the compounds bicyclic scaffold is a dihydropyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine, where the nitrogen in the pyrrole ring is dimethyl-substituted to form a quaternary ammonium salt center, ensuring a fixed positive charge regardless of pH, the C6 position is linked via a methylene bridge to a meta-trifluoromethyl substituted aniline, the incorporation of the trifluoromethyl group enhances the molecules lipophilicity, and the pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine scaffold is common in many neuroactive drugs and GPCR ligands, consequently, this compound may be studied as a potential receptor ligand or ion channel modulator targeting systems relevant to neurological and psychiatric disorders.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 2-amino-1,7-dihydropurin-6-one, also referred to as Guanine (G), is the basic purine nucleobase and a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that also acts as a natural ligand for the xpt-pbuX riboswitch and binds to aptamer with high affinity; its N1, N2, O6, N7 and N9 positions interact closely with the RNA-binding pocket through seven hydrogen bonds, inducing aptamer to fold and stabilize the P1 stem structure, promoting the formation of transcription terminators on the downstream expression platform, thereby shutting down the expression of purine transport and metabolism genes such as xpt-pbuX, and achieving purine homeostasis feedback regulation, it is one of the four principal bases found in DNA and RNA, and a product of purine metabolism, forms three hydrogen bonds with Cytosine (C) and is crucial for genetic information transfer, while the base itself has no direct clinical use, it is a foundational molecule for studying gene expression, DNA repair, and cell cycle regulation, and is the core scaffold for many antiviral and anticancer drugs.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: (E,2S,7S)-2,7-diamino-N,N-bis[(2S)-1-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]amino]-1-oxopropan-2-yl]-N,N-dipropyloct-4-enediamide, also referred to as Compound 10E, is a large, symmetrical peptidomimetic that notably lacks large aromatic intercalating groups like benzo[g]quinoline (it is a truncated core peptide analog lacking both benzo[g]quinoline intercalating groups), it exhibits no detectable binding to the HIV-1 FSS RNA in SPR assays, and although it showed a modest, non-specific reduction in viral infectivity at high concentrations, this effect is attributed to off-target activity rather than specific interaction with the FSS, serving as an important negative control that demonstrates the antiviral effects observed for 4Z and 5E require high-affinity RNA binding mediated by the extended aromatic scaffolds; the molecule is composed primarily of amino acid derivatives and polyamines, with an eight-carbon backbone containing a trans (E) double bond, providing a relatively extended and fixed conformation, and it is symmetrically flanked by alanine-phenylalanine-polyamine peptidic chains, possessing multiple primary and secondary amino groups that confer a strong polycationic character, this structure is more inclined to function as a cell-penetrating peptide or a selective inhibitor of proteases/receptors, its polyamine nature allows for efficient interaction with cell membranes or negatively charged intracellular proteins, and potential real-world applications include drug delivery systems or the development of highly selective enzyme inhibitors.

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[4-[(E)-2-[1-methyl-4-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)quinolin-1-ium-2-yl]ethenyl]phenyl]morpholine, also referred to as Compound 22 (a green-yellow 1-methylquinolinium derivative), is similar to 2-[(E)-2-(1H-indol-2-yl)ethenyl]-1-methyl-4-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)quinolin-1-ium, this is a quinolinium cationic dye, but the indole group is replaced by a morpholine-substituted phenyl group, with Compound 22 featuring a 4-morpholinostyryl group at the 2-position and a 4-methylpiperazin-1-yl group at the 4-position of the quinoline ring; the quinolinium core provides the positive charge, and the conjugated ethynyl linker confers fluorescence, these molecules are important biological tools used as DNA/RNA binding fluorescent probes, analogous to dyes like Sybr Green, and Compound 22 has been shown via SPR assay to have affinity for A10-RNA-Wt (ADAM10 mRNAs G-quadruplex sequence), via dual-luciferase reporter assay to enhance ADAM10 translation, and to possess cytotoxicity, while its potential activity is as a cell imaging and nucleic acid detection tool, or as a cytotoxic antineoplastic agent due to its tight binding to DNA.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: (3-amino-6-methylthieno[2,3-b]pyridin-2-yl)-morpholin-4-ylmethanone, also referred to as Compound 14, is a thienopyridine carboxamide derivative with a morphinyl side chain modified from initial active analogs, containing thienopyridine and morpholine heterocyclic rings. Its structure is based on the thienopyridine parent skeleton: a thiophene ring is fused with a pyridine ring, substituted with an amino group at C3, a methyl group at C6, and a morpholinylcarbonyl group at C2. In terms of biological function, thienopyridine compounds are known for their diverse pharmacological activities, including anticancer, antiviral, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial effects, and this compound binds HIV-1 TAR RNA and RRE RNA key for viral replication, targeting HIV-1 infection by interacting with these regulatory RNAs, showing moderate affinity and no further functional validation for anti-HIV-1 activity.

Small_molecule_information: 24-methyl-5,7,18,20-tetraoxa-24-azoniahexacyclo[11.11.0.02,10.04,8.014,22.017,21]tetracosa-1(24),2,4(8),9,11,13,15,17(21),22-nonaene, also referred to as Sanguinarine, is a highly complex, polycyclic cationic compound, which can be chemically identified as the N-methyl derivative of a macrocyclic tetraoxaza-heteroatom system, and its molecular structure is dominated by its extensive ring fusion as indicated by its IUPAC name, and Sanguinarine is nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that can specifically bind to the binding pocket of Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA, inducing significant structural changes in the RNA, Sanguinarine is also a benzophenanthridine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and pro-apoptotic effects, it targets multiple cellular pathways, including NF-B, and is studied for anticancer applications despite potential toxicity concerns, the compounds formula, C21H16NO4, reveals a structure containing 21 carbon atoms, 4 oxygen atoms, and a single nitrogen atom that carries a permanent positive charge (azonia) due to methylation, the core hexacyclic structure suggests a rigid, cage-like framework featuring two major 12-membered rings bridged by various fused smaller rings (implied by the [11.11.0.02,10.04,8.014,22.017,21] nomenclature), leading to a highly planar and stiff molecular geometry, the presence of four oxygen atoms and nine double bonds (nonaene) indicates extensive unsaturation, and the presence of oxygen atoms at positions 5, 7, 18, and 20 suggests they are likely incorporated into ether or furan-like structures, strongly suggesting this molecule is an analog of macrocyclic dyes or natural products with rigid, fluorescent properties, potentially related to porphyrins or cryptands in shape, although Sanguinarine has a high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA, it fails to activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, possibly due to its toxicity to cells, nevertheless, Sanguinarine, as one of the potential ligands, provides important clues for studying the functional mechanism of this riboswitch, due to its fixed positive charge and large, planar aromatic system, this compound is highly likely to function as an intercalating agent for nucleic acids, making it a potential DNA/RNA binding fluorescent probe for biological imaging, or a promising scaffold for anti-cancer or antimicrobial drug development, where its rigidity could confer high target specificity.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (2S)-5-hydroxy-2-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxyphenyl)-7-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-[[(2R,3R,4R,5R,6S)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxymethyl]oxan-2-yl]oxy-2,3-dihydrochromen-4-one, also referred to as Hesperidin, is a flavonoid glycoside with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects commonly found in citrus fruits, and is an extremely complex natural product flavonoid O-diglycoside with a dihydroflavone (flavanone) core substituted at the 7-position with a diglycoside unit (likely involving glucose and rhamnose); the numerous phenolic hydroxyl groups and the bulky glycoside moiety are the primary drivers of its activity, and in practice, such compounds are key functional components in herbal medicines, demonstrating potent antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and free radical scavenging capabilities, while due to its glycosidic structure, it typically has high water solubility and specific oral bioavailability (via gut microbial hydrolysis), and it is widely studied for cardiovascular benefits as well as cardioprotective, antiviral, and hepatoprotective effects, making it a focal point in natural product research.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 2-N,9-N-bis[(E)-(1-benzylquinolin-1-ium-4-yl)methylideneamino]-1,10-phenanthroline-2,9-dicarboxamide dibromide, also referred to as PhenDH3, is an isomer of 2-N,9-N-bis[(E)-(1-benzylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]-1,10-phenanthroline-2,9-dicarboxamide dibromide, differing in that its quaternized quinoline side chains are attached through the 4-position instead of the 6-position of the quinoline to the 1,10-phenanthroline-2,9-dicarboxamide core1-G4EBNA1DNA

RNA information: NA 4 (Drosha and adjacent 515) is an RNA fragment mimicking the Drosha processing site and its flanking region in pri-miR-515. Its core structural feature is the simultaneous inclusion of the Drosha processing site motif unique to pri-miR-515 and the adjacent Compound 1-binding motif, and the combination of these two motifs provides conditions for dual-site binding.; RNA 4 (Drosha and adjacent 515) is an RNA fragment mimicking the Drosha processing site and its flanking region in pri-miR-515. Its core structural feature is the simultaneous inclusion of the Drosha processing site motif unique to pri-miR-515 and the adjacent Compound 1-binding motif, and the combination of these two motifs provides conditions for dual-site binding.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[5-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]-N-[3-[4-(hydroxymethyl)triazol-1-yl]propyl]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 1b, is a triazole derivative of Compound 1 that is formed by coupling azide-containing Compound 1 with propargyl alcohol via Cu-catalyzed azide-alkyne click chemistry to introduce a triazole group into its structure and serves as a control to evaluate the effect of the triazole unit on binding affinity; it is a large, multi-domain molecule featuring a core bis-benzimidazole moiety, which is a classic DNA minor groove binder, and a 2,6-di-tert-butylphenol fragment, a renowned antioxidant pharmacophore, with its structure linked to a hydroxymethyl-triazole group, this compound is likely designed to explore dual-action pharmacology, where it could act as a nucleic acid binding agent for cellular toxicity while simultaneously offering antioxidant protection via the di-tert-butylphenol unit, and in reality, it could serve as a valuable chemical tool for investigating DNA-drug interactions or as a potential therapeutic agent in complex diseases like cancer.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 5-(diaminomethylideneamino)-2-[[2-[[2-[(5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl)amino]acetyl]amino]-3-phenylpropanoyl]amino]pentanoic acid. This is a complex peptide-heterocycle conjugate. The core is the cationic benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium scaffold, linked via a diglycyl spacer to a derivative of the arginine-phenylalanine peptide segment. This structure exemplifies targeted drug design, aiming to use the peptide segments specificity to guide the drug to specific cellular receptors or transport systems. The practical application of this compound is in targeted anti-cancer therapy. The polycyclic cationic moiety (benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline) acts as the cytotoxic carrier, while the peptide portion may recognize overexpressed receptors on the cancer cell surface, achieving selective enrichment in tumor tissue and potentially reducing toxicity to normal cells.

RNA information: The RRE RNA is a highly structured regulatory element from HIV-1, with its three-dimensional architecture determined by specific base-pairing and loop regions. Its functional role is to specifically bind the viral Rev protein, facilitating the nuclear export of unspliced viral mRNAs—a critical step in viral replication. The binding pocket is highly conserved, with key residues including G70 and C61 being essential for molecular recognition, as mutations significantly disrupt ligand binding. This RNA serves as a promising target for antiviral therapeutics, as demonstrated by its selective interaction with synthetic ligands like thienopyridine derivatives, which block the essential Rev-RRE interactio
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-N-(1H-benzimidazol-2-yl)-6-methylthieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 13, is a thieno[2,3-b]pyridine-based small molecule specifically designed to target the HIV-1 RRE RNA, which is based on the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine core, with the carboxamide at the 2-position linked to a 1H-benzimidazol-2-yl group; it achieves binding by forming key hydrogen bonds between its amide linkage and the phosphate group of nucleotide G70, and mutagenesis studies confirm the criticality of residues G70 and C49 for this interaction, as their mutation severely disrupts binding activity, making it a pioneering RRE-binding ligand and a valuable structural template for developing novel antiviral agents targeting RNA-protein interactions, while benzimidazole is an important bicyclic heterocyclic pharmacophore found in a wide array of drugs, including antiviral, antifungal, antihypertensive agents, and proton pump inhibitors, and linking benzimidazole via an amide bond to the thienopyridine scaffold is intended to develop molecules with multiple biological activities, and the heterocyclic nature of benzimidazole makes it an excellent hydrogen bond donor/acceptor, crucial for stable complex formation with enzyme or receptor active sites, and this molecule is likely a candidate for a kinase inhibitor or receptor antagonist, with potential antiviral or anti-cancer applications.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a short, genetically conserved stem–bulge–loop hairpin found at the 5′ end of every viral transcript.  Functional constructs range from minimal 29-nt fragments (containing the essential bulge and loop) up to the canonical 52–59-nt forms seen in full-length genomes; in all cases the active fold is the same: a double-stranded stem interrupted by a three-base pyrimidine bulge (commonly UCU) and capped by a six-nucleotide loop.  This bulge-and-loop motif first captures an arginine-rich segment of the viral Tat protein, inducing a local conformational switch that allows Tat to recruit the host Cyclin T1–CDK9 (P-TEFb) complex.  The resultant Tat–TAR–P-TEFb assembly converts RNA polymerase II from abortive to highly processive elongation, boosting viral gene expression >100-fold, and the same RNA structure also facilitates strand-transfer during reverse transcription.  Because the bulge–loop signature is indispensable and sequence-invariant across strains, TAR acts as a natural “riboswitch-like” sensor that tunes the balance between transcription and genome replication, offering a structurally small yet functionally critical target for therapeutic intervention.
Small_molecule_information: 2,6-diacetyl-7,9-dihydroxy-9b-methyl-4,4a,5a,6,7,8,9,9a-octahydrodibenzofuran-1,3-dione, also referred to as usnic acid (CHO), is a complex, highly oxidized dibenzofuran derivative featuring multiple acetyl, hydroxyl, and ketone substituents as well as several chiral centers, and although its octahydrodibenzofuran core is extensively functionalized, it retains the rigid, planar polyketide-derived dibenzofuran framework that enables it to act as a non-phosphorylated, redox-neutral analog of flavin mononucleotide capable of engaging the HIV-1 TAR RNA bulge through -stacking and a preserved hydrogen-bond donor/acceptor triad, thereby functioning as a naturally derived scaffold for developing next-generation antivirals that target viral RNA instead of host proteins.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: [[(2R,3R,4R,5R)-5-(6-aminopurin-9-yl)-3-hydroxy-4-phosphonooxyoxolan-2-yl]methoxy-hydroxyphosphoryl] [(2R,3S,4R,5R)-5-(3-carbamoylpyridin-1-ium-1-yl)-3,4-dihydroxyoxolan-2-yl]methyl phosphate, also referred to as -nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP), is Nicotinamide Adenine Dinucleotide Phosphate (NADP), or its reduced form, NADPH, and -nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP) is a phosphorylated nucleotide coenzyme with a structure comprising a nicotinamide ring, adenine base, two ribose sugars, and three phosphate groups (with an extra phosphate on adenines ribose vs. NAD), its nicotinamide moiety acts as a redox active site, accepting/donating electrons, NADPH is the source of reducing power essential for biosynthesis and protecting cells from oxidative stress, the molecule is a key component in biochemical and pharmacological research, involved in a wide range of metabolic regulation, functionally, it mediates anabolic reactions (e.g., photosynthesis, lipid/nucleotide synthesis), serving as a key electron carrier in cellular redox pathways and supporting biosynthetic processes by providing reducing power in the form of NADPH. Small_molecule_information: 2-amino-4-oxo-3,7-dihydropyrrolo[2,3-d]pyrimidine-5-carbonitrile, also referred to as preQ0 (7-aminomethyl-7-deazaguanine), is a compound containing the pyrrolo[2,3-d]pyrimidine scaffold, which is a structural analog of natural nucleobases (like adenine and guanine), with the formula C7H5N5O, and preQ0 (7-aminomethyl-7-deazaguanine) is a modified purine nucleobase precursor to preQ1 and queuosine, featuring a deazapurine core with an aminomethyl group at the 7-position (replacing the typical purine N7), structurally, it retains the bicyclic purine scaffold but has a unique carbon-linked amine side chain, enabling specific riboswitch recognition, this compound is generally designed as an antimetabolite or nucleotide metabolism inhibitor, its biological activity stems from its ability to mimic natural substrates, potentially interfering with DNA and RNA synthesis by incorporating into nucleic acid chains or inhibiting key enzymes, functionally, preQ0 acts as a key intermediate in queuosine biosynthesis, binds dedicated preQ0 riboswitches to regulate related metabolic genes, and mediates bacterial gene expression via ligand-induced RNA conformational changes, ensuring proper tRNA modification and translational fidelity, it is a focus in the development of broad-spectrum antiviral, anticancer, and immunosuppressive agents. tRNA

Small_molecule_information: 1,7-dihydropurin-6-one, also referred to as Hypoxanthine (or its tautomer), is a naturally occurring oxopurine nucleobase and nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound in which a keto oxygen replaces the amino group of adenine at position 6 of the purine ring, and is one of the most fundamental derivatives of the purine scaffold; as an intermediate in purine salvage and catabolism it is ubiquitous across all cell types, and is also a key intermediate in the human purine metabolism, being the product of adenine deamination, which is subsequently oxidized by xanthine oxidase to xanthine, and finally metabolized to uric acid, while lacking both the N-3 methyl and the N-7 proton required by the TCT8-4 RNA aptamer, it binds far more weakly than theophylline, underscoring the RNAs evolved selectivity for the 1,3-dimethyl-xanthine scaffold; as an endogenous metabolite, hypoxanthine plays crucial physiological roles in the cell, for instance, by being a component of the inosine nucleotide, clinically, understanding hypoxanthine metabolism is essential for the diagnosis and treatment of gout (linked to abnormal uric acid metabolism) and certain hereditary enzyme deficiencies, and in reality, hypoxanthine itself is frequently used in biochemical research as a component of culture media or to study purine salvage pathways and related enzyme activities.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 1-ethyl-2-methyl-3-phenylquinazolin-1-ium-4-one, also referred to as CP8 which is based on a quinazolinone core with N-ethyl, N-methyl, and phenyl substitutions, is a quinazolinone quaternary ammonium salt; the substituted quinazolinone scaffold of this compound showed measurable binding but was ultimately superseded by compounds with superior affinity and selectivity, and quaternary ammonium salts often possess broad-spectrum antibacterial activity by non-specifically disrupting bacterial membranes, making this a potential antimicrobial agent.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 9-[(4aR,6R,7R,7aS)-2,7-dihydroxy-2-oxo-4a,6,7,7a-tetrahydro-4H-furo[3,2-d][1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin-6-yl]-2-(methylamino)-1H-purin-6-one, also referred to as N2-methylguanosine-3,5-cyclic monophosphate (2-Me-cGMP), is a highly modified nucleoside analogue, and 2-Me-cGMP is a non-azaaromatic cyclic nucleotide with a guanine base (methylated at N2), a ribose ring, and a 3,5-cyclic phosphate group that lacks planar hydrophobic azaaromatic features, binds weakly to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) and fails to activate riboswitch-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis, potentially functioning in cyclic nucleotide signaling rather than azaaromatic sensing; its nucleobase component, 2-methylamino-1H-purin-6-one, is a derivative of Guanine, the most striking feature is the sugar moiety, which forms a complex bicyclic phosphate ester structure, specifically a fused [1,3,2]dioxaphosphinin ring containing a phosphate bond, this unique cyclic phosphate structure is intended to mimic or modify the activity of natural nucleotides, such as GTP or cAMP, and as nucleoside analogues can interfere with nucleic acid metabolism, this compound could be researched as an antiviral or anticancer drug in reality, by acting as a false substrate to inhibit key polymerases or signaling enzymes, such as GTP cyclases, although its high polarity may limit cell permeability.

Small_molecule_information: 3,5-diamino-6-chloro-N-(diaminomethylidene)pyrazine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Amiloride, is a well-known potassium-sparing diuretic that blocks epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the kidneys, with the molecule acting by reversibly blocking the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) in the renal collecting ducts, thereby reducing sodium reabsorption and fluid retention. Clinically, Amiloride is used to treat hypertension, congestive heart failure, and edema, and is often combined with other diuretics to prevent hypokalemia.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: (2S)-5-hydroxy-2-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxyphenyl)-7-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-[[(2R,3R,4R,5R,6S)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxymethyl]oxan-2-yl]oxy-2,3-dihydrochromen-4-one, also referred to as Hesperidin, is a flavonoid glycoside with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects commonly found in citrus fruits, and is an extremely complex natural product flavonoid O-diglycoside with a dihydroflavone (flavanone) core substituted at the 7-position with a diglycoside unit (likely involving glucose and rhamnose); the numerous phenolic hydroxyl groups and the bulky glycoside moiety are the primary drivers of its activity, and in practice, such compounds are key functional components in herbal medicines, demonstrating potent antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and free radical scavenging capabilities, while due to its glycosidic structure, it typically has high water solubility and specific oral bioavailability (via gut microbial hydrolysis), and it is widely studied for cardiovascular benefits as well as cardioprotective, antiviral, and hepatoprotective effects, making it a focal point in natural product research.

RNA information: r(CUG)₁₂ (r(5′CUG/3′GUC)12) is a pathogenic RNA motif modeling myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1)-causing repeats. Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with 12 CUG repeats, featuring a C-G paired stem and periodic 1×1 UU internal loops that create specific binding pockets. Functionally, it sequesters MBNL1 splicing regulator to disrupt pre-mRNA splicing (DM1’s core pathogenesis), serves as a non-cognate control for binding specificity assays, and is a target for small molecules that displace MBNL1 to restore RNA processing.
Small_molecule_information: 1-N,4-N-bis(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)benzene-1,4-dicarboxamide also referred to as Compound P2 (a pentamidine-like small molecule), also belongs to the bis-amidines class of derivatives, lacking one amino group compared to 2-amino-1-N,4-N-bis(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)benzene-1,4-dicarboxamide, and P2 is a next-generation ligand for the pathogenic r(CUG)exp RNA that underlies myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) discovered by 3-D shape similarity screening, which preserves the twin benzamidine cations of pentamidine but carries an extended hydrophobic linker that improves its shape match to the major groove of the 5CUG/3GUC hairpin repeat, can dissociate MBNL1 from r(CUG)12 in a TR-FRET displacement assay with potency superior to pentamidine yet is less selective than P1, binding equally to RNAs that contain three or more DM1 motifs, the human A-site or cTNT pre-mRNA, and in DM1 cell models can partially shift a bichromatic MBNL1 exon 5 reporter toward the normal exon-skipped DsRed signal though cytotoxicity becomes evident at higher doses, exemplifying how computational shape similarity can rapidly evolve an antimicrobial template into a low-micromolar chemical probe for DM1 RNA that may serve as a stepping-stone toward in-vivo-compatible DM1 therapeutics; bis-amidine compounds are often used to inhibit serine proteases or as DNA/RNA binding agents, showing potential for research in antiparasitic diseases (such as trypanosomiasis, malaria) and antifungal applications, and the specific terephthalamide bis-amidine structure suggests that the molecule may have the potential to act as a novel antibacterial or antiproliferative agent, but detailed public biological activity data for this specific molecule is not available.

Small_molecule_information: 24-methyl-5,7,18,20-tetraoxa-24-azoniahexacyclo[11.11.0.02,10.04,8.014,22.017,21]tetracosa-1(24),2,4(8),9,11,13,15,17(21),22-nonaene, also referred to as Sanguinarine, is a highly complex, polycyclic cationic compound, which can be chemically identified as the N-methyl derivative of a macrocyclic tetraoxaza-heteroatom system, and its molecular structure is dominated by its extensive ring fusion as indicated by its IUPAC name, and Sanguinarine is nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound that can specifically bind to the binding pocket of Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA, inducing significant structural changes in the RNA, Sanguinarine is also a benzophenanthridine alkaloid with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and pro-apoptotic effects, it targets multiple cellular pathways, including NF-B, and is studied for anticancer applications despite potential toxicity concerns, the compounds formula, C21H16NO4, reveals a structure containing 21 carbon atoms, 4 oxygen atoms, and a single nitrogen atom that carries a permanent positive charge (azonia) due to methylation, the core hexacyclic structure suggests a rigid, cage-like framework featuring two major 12-membered rings bridged by various fused smaller rings (implied by the [11.11.0.02,10.04,8.014,22.017,21] nomenclature), leading to a highly planar and stiff molecular geometry, the presence of four oxygen atoms and nine double bonds (nonaene) indicates extensive unsaturation, and the presence of oxygen atoms at positions 5, 7, 18, and 20 suggests they are likely incorporated into ether or furan-like structures, strongly suggesting this molecule is an analog of macrocyclic dyes or natural products with rigid, fluorescent properties, potentially related to porphyrins or cryptands in shape, although Sanguinarine has a high binding affinity for 108yjdF RNA, it fails to activate gene expression regulated by 108yjdF RNA, possibly due to its toxicity to cells, nevertheless, Sanguinarine, as one of the potential ligands, provides important clues for studying the functional mechanism of this riboswitch, due to its fixed positive charge and large, planar aromatic system, this compound is highly likely to function as an intercalating agent for nucleic acids, making it a potential DNA/RNA binding fluorescent probe for biological imaging, or a promising scaffold for anti-cancer or antimicrobial drug development, where its rigidity could confer high target specificity.

Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-9-methoxy-2-(4-methylbenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 8, is a rigid and structurally complex derivative of chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one (a fused chromenone-pyridine ring system) that features a methoxy group on the chromene ring, multiple substituents including 3-amino, 4-phenyl, 9-methoxy, and 2-(4-methylbenzoyl), and exhibited moderate RNA-binding behavior which helped illustrate the importance of substituent positioning on biological activity; Chromenone-based compounds are common in natural products and exhibit broad bioactivities, particularly anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anticancer effects, and this highly substituted, complex heterocyclic system is often designed as a kinase inhibitor or receptor antagonist, with the benzoyl and phenyl substituents providing the necessary hydrophobic interactions and spatial orientation for protein target binding, while in drug research, such molecules are pursued for their unique structure and potential antitumor activity, possibly by interfering with the cell cycle or inhibiting specific signaling enzymes.

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 16-[3-(3-fluoropyridin-1-ium-1-yl)propoxy]-17-methoxy-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaene is a fluorinated analog of 17-methoxy-16-[3-(3-methylpyridin-1-ium-1-yl)propoxy]-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.02,10.04,8.015,20]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaene, with a fluorine atom introduced at the 3-position of the pyridinium ring. The introduction of fluorine is a common strategy in medicinal chemistry to optimize lead compounds, as fluorine can significantly affect a molecules lipophilicity, metabolic stability, and electronegativity, thereby fine-tuning its interaction with targets. 16-[3-(3-fluoropyridin-1-ium-1-yl)propoxy]-17-methoxy-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.02,10.04,8.015,20]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaene remains a dicationic compound, maintaining a strong propensity for membrane activity. Functionally, it is expected to retain the antimicrobial properties of its dicationic parent core, but the introduction of fluorine may improve its pharmacokinetic (ADME) properties, such as enhanced bioavailability and stability in vivo.

RNA information: r(AUAU): Functioning as another critical model hairpin, r(AUAU) contains a stem with repeating 5'-AU/3'-UA base pair steps. This structural motif is of particular interest as it directly mimics the periodic AU base pair steps found in the pathogenic r(AUUCU) repeat expansion responsible for Spinocerebellar Ataxia Type 10 (SCA10). In binding assays, this RNA consistently showed high affinity for the lead compounds, especially molecule 2 and its dimeric derivative 2AU-2, confirming it as a high-affinity target. Its design was crucial for identifying small molecules that could be developed to target the disease-causing RNA structure selectively.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)furan-2-yl]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 2 (DB75, furamidine), is a bis-imidamide compound where the two benzamidine groups are connected by a furan ring, with the formula C18H16N4O, and it is also a bis-cationic diphenylfuran derivative with a furamidine chromophore and smaller amidine end-groups; the introduction of the furan ring imparts some flexibility to the molecule, allowing for conformational adjustments during DNA binding, and this compound can selectively bind AU base pairs with positive cooperativity, targeting r(AUUCU) repeats and protecting AU sites from RNase cleavage, can bind HIV-1 TAR RNAs GCUCU motif via shape-driven recognition, displace Tat, block trans-activation, stabilize TAR, and has anti-PCP activity/DNA minor-groove binding, with no cytotoxicity below 30 M and serving as a TAR-targeting benchmark; these heterocyclic diamidines are a focus in the development of antiparasitic drugs, leveraging their strong DNA binding ability to effectively inhibit the growth of protozoa like Trypanosoma, and also show potential for antifungal and anticancer therapy.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Quercetin, is a natural flavonol compound and one of the most common and widely studied flavonoids in nature, with the formula C15H10O7. Its structure features hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and a catechol moiety (two adjacent hydroxyl groups) on the B ring (3 and 4 positions). Quercetin is abundant in various plant-based foods, including onions, apples, tea, berries, and red wine. In real-world biological activity, Quercetin exhibits outstanding pharmacological properties: it is a powerful antioxidant and free-radical scavenger, effective at inhibiting lipid peroxidation, and also possesses anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties; it also acts as an effective anti-inflammatory agent by inhibiting histamine release, modulating immune cell function, and suppressing various inflammation-related enzymes (such as cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase), and has been shown to interact with G-quadruplex DNA and may modulate RNA-related pathways. Furthermore, Quercetin is studied for its anti-allergic, cardioprotective, antiviral, and potential anticancer properties, and is widely used and researched as a dietary supplement or a component in natural medicine.

RNA information: BS-xpt(A24U) is a mutant of the Bacillus subtilis BS-xpt guanine riboswitch, with adenine at position 24 (aptamer core, near the ligand-binding site) mutated to uracil. Structurally, it disrupts wild-type core folding, as position 24's nucleotide requirement depends on sequence context. Functionally, it has no detectable 2AP binding, impairs guanine binding, and likely reduces in vivo transcriptional termination regulation efficiency vs wild-type BS-xpt.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.

Small_molecule_information: [9-(2-carboxyphenyl)-6-(diethylamino)xanthen-3-ylidene]-diethylazanium, also referred to as Rhodamine, is a xanthene derivative, typically classified as a Rhodamine-type fluorescent dye. The structure is based on a xanthene ring, with the molecular formula C28H31N2O3. This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and is known for its exceptionally high fluorescence intensity and photostability. In biological function, this compound and its analogs (like Rhodamine B) are widely used as fluorescent tracers in biological and medical research, employed for labeling proteins, antibodies, or for cell counting and sorting in flow cytometry, and it is also a fluorescent dye used for staining and imaging in biological research that labels cellular structures while its interaction with nucleic acids (DNA/RNA) is nonspecific and primarily for visualization purposes.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[11-[3-(diethylamino)propylamino]-5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-7-yl]oxy]ethyl-trimethylazanium diiodide is a benzofuroquinoline bis-quaternary ammonium salt derivative, structurally analogous to 3-[[11-[3-(diethylamino)propylamino]-5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-7-yl]oxy]propyl-trimethylazanium diiodide, but the 7-position side chain is a shorter ethoxy-linked trimethylammonium group (molecular formula C30H41N4O22I). This compound exhibits a planar conjugated system and two positive charges. The shortened side chain is likely intended to modify the molecules spatial presentation on the biological target, retaining its high potential as a nucleic acid binding agent.

Small_molecule_information: (2R,3R,4R,5R)-4-amino-2-[(2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(1R,2S)-2-amino-6-(aminomethyl)cyclohexyl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-5-methyloxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as Sisomicin Sulphate, is a complex aminoglycoside compound featuring multiple sugar rings and an aminocyclohexitol structure linked by glycosidic bonds. The molecule contains numerous amine (NH2) groups, rendering it polycationic at physiological pH. Aminoglycosides are a critical class of broad-spectrum antibiotics that function by irreversibly binding to the 30S subunit of the bacterial ribosome, disrupting protein synthesis by binding to rRNA, inhibiting protein synthesis. Similar to clinical antibiotics like Streptomycin or Gentamicin, this molecule is primarily used to treat severe Gram-negative bacterial infections, though its therapeutic use must consider inherent ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity side effects.

RNA information: The influenza A virus RNA promoter features a bent A-form helical structure with a dynamic (A–A)–U internal loop that serves as a specific binding site for the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) and nucleoprotein complex. This unique structural motif exhibits conformational flexibility that facilitates recognition by viral replication machinery while maintaining overall A-form geometry. The promoter's distinctive architecture and dynamic properties are essential for initiating viral RNA synthesis, making it a critical functional element in the influenza replication cycle and a promising target for antiviral therapeutic development; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Its structure, determined by NMR spectroscopy, adopts a panhandle-like conformation characterized by an A-form helix. A key structural feature is a unique (A-A)-U internal loop and a C-A mismatch pair. Furthermore, the promoter exhibits a significant bend of approximately 46 ± 10° near the viral RNA initiation site. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. The high conservation of its sequence across strains and its intolerance to mutations, which are detrimental to viral replication, establish the influenza RNA promoter as a novel and promising druggable target for antiviral development.; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Small_molecule_information: [4-(4-amino-6,7-dimethoxyquinazolin-2-yl)piperazin-1-yl]-(furan-2-yl)methanone, also referred to as Compound 14 (a DPQ (compound 1) derivative with the piperazines secondary amine modified where Rfuran), is a compound that is synthesized via one-step coupling, binds the influenza A RNA promoter (causing U27 imino proton line broadening) and has improved anti-influenza activity but is toxic to MDCK-HA cells; this compound features a furan-2-carbonyl (furan-2-ylmethanone) side chain, the furan ring, a five-membered aromatic heterocycle, introduces unique steric and electronic properties and an additional hydrogen bond acceptor site, furan derivatives are commonly found in compounds with anti-inflammatory and anticancer activities, while the main activity remains 1-adrenergic receptor antagonism, the furan ring acts as an auxiliary pharmacophore, allowing for more specific interactions within the binding pocket, potentially optimizing its selectivity for a particular 1-receptor subtype.

RNA information: The r(CUG)₁₀ sequence represents a pathogenic-length model for expanded CUG repeats and folds into a stable hairpin with regularly spaced U–U mismatches. Structurally, it provides multiple equivalent binding sites, enabling one ligand to associate with each trinucleotide repeat and producing cooperative binding. This repeat length captures essential features of toxic RNA found in myotonic dystrophy, including the ability to sequester MBNL1 and form nuclear foci. Functionally, r(CUG)₁₀ is the most relevant in vitro construct for evaluating selective small-molecule recognition, since its length allows the manifestation of cooperative interactions that mimic disease-relevant RNA–protein complexes.
Small_molecule_information: (Z,2R,11R)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,11-bis[[(2S)-2-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)-propylamino]propanoyl]amino]dodec-6-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 10 (2012), is a structurally massive cis (Z) peptidomimetic dimer that incorporates an extended hydrocarbon bridge via pentenyl glycine residues, producing greater linker length and conformational flexibility which allows the benzo[g]quinoline groups to adopt more favorable orientations for engaging RNA repeats, and this structural extension enhances the ability of the compound to match the geometry of expanded repeat hairpins; functionally, it exhibits strong activity in cellular models, efficiently penetrating cells and rescuing functional phenotypes caused by toxic RNA, and it demonstrates that tuning linker length and flexibility can significantly improve recognition efficiency and biological performance, making it a promising scaffold for therapeutic exploration, while its Z-configuration of the core twelve-carbon chain forces a rigid U-shaped bend in the main chain, significantly altering its overall spatial conformation, and this conformational change is critical for the molecules biological activity as it determines the relative distance and orientation of the two active units (benzo[g]quinoline/lysine side chains) in space, and this specific bent conformation may make it more suitable for binding to DNA regions with a particular curvature or to oligomeric protein receptors, and the molecule maintains the powerful polycationic character and the DNA binding potential of the benzo[g]quinoline groups, thus this compound is designed as a crucial molecule for studying the conformation-activity relationship of large polycationic peptidomimetics, with potential applications as a highly specific anti-cancer agent or gene therapy vector.
